specification.topic-statement: The configuration loader assembles application settings from compiled-in defaults, an optional system prompt file, and environment variable overrides, producing a single configuration record used at startup.

scope.in-scope: The configuration data contract, the three-tier source priority order, default values for all fields, the system prompt file loading and validation rules, environment variable override rules with field-specific validation, and silent-rejection behavior for invalid overrides.
scope.boundary: How configuration values are used by agents, providers, and the database connection is handled by those subsystems and is out of scope here.
API key retrieval from the keychain or environment is handled by the credential loading subsystem and is out of scope here.

field.name: AI model
field.note: identifier for the default language model

field.name: AI max completion tokens
field.note: maximum tokens in an LLM response

field.name: AI system message
field.default: You are a personal agent and are operating inside the Ikigai orchestration platform.
field.note: default system prompt when no file or pinned documents are set

field.name: network address
field.note: address on which the control socket server listens

field.name: network port
field.note: port on which the control socket server listens

field.name: max tool turns
field.note: maximum tool call iterations in a single agent conversation

field.name: max output size
field.note: maximum byte length of tool output captured from a child process

field.name: history size
field.note: number of command history entries retained

field.name: default provider
field.note: identifier for the default LLM provider

field.name: sliding context tokens
field.note: token budget for the sliding context window

behavior.loading-sequence:
Configuration loading proceeds in four steps executed once at startup:
1. A zeroed configuration record is allocated. 2. All fields are set to their compiled-in default values. 3. A system prompt file is checked and, if valid, its content replaces the default system message. 4. Environment variables are checked and, if valid, their values replace the corresponding fields.
The result is a fully populated configuration record where every field is set.
behavior.system-prompt-file:
Before defaults are applied, the loader checks for a system prompt file at a fixed path within the application data directory. If the file exists and passes validation, its content is used as the system message, taking priority over the compiled-in default.
Validation rules for the system prompt file:
- The file must exist and be readable. - The file must not be empty (zero bytes). - The file must not exceed 1,024 bytes. - The read must complete successfully (no partial reads).
If any validation step fails, the file is silently ignored and the compiled-in default system message is used.
The file content is read before defaults are applied, but the replacement takes place as part of the defaults step, making file content the effective default for the system message field.
behavior.environment-variable-overrides:
After defaults are applied, environment variables may override six fields. Each variable is checked independently; failure of one does not affect the others.
**String fields** (database host, database name, database user, default provider): the environment variable value must be a non-empty string. Empty strings are treated as absent and the default is retained. Valid values replace the current field value.
**Integer fields** (database port, sliding context token budget): the environment variable value is parsed as a decimal integer. Two additional constraints apply: - The database port must be in the range 1–65535. - The token budget must be a positive (non-zero) integer.
If parsing fails or the constraint is not met, the override is silently ignored and the default is retained.
**Default provider**: checked via a dedicated lookup that queries the environment variable and falls back to the configured value if absent.
behavior.silent-rejection:
Overrides that fail validation are never reported to the user. The application continues with the default or previously set value. This applies to: - Empty string environment variables - Unparseable numeric environment variables - Numeric values outside the valid range
A misconfigured environment variable (for example, a non-numeric value for database port) is silently ignored. Users who expect an override to take effect will see the default behavior with no indication of why.
behavior.allocation-failure: Memory allocation failures during configuration loading cause an immediate process abort. There is no partial-configuration recovery path.
